title: Connector-Level Packing Codes
text: Connector-Level Packing Codes The symbolic values for the connector level packing codes are defined in the unidbm.h include file provided by Uniface. Note: pLC, pLU and pLR are not defined, since no packing code can be redefined to these codes. The term native encoding refers to the hardware architecture, not necessarily to the DBMS. The following packing codes have an explicit length indicator: pC pU pR pY1 through pY32 pZ1 through pZ8 This is the same as the field uflen in the field list. For all other packing codes, the length is implied by the packing code. pC Character string. The byte values 1 through 255 must be supported. pU Uniface TRX transfer format character string. The byte values 32 through 126 must be supported. The Uniface TRX transfer format is a platform-independent and DBMS-independent format for data exchange, used prior to Uniface 9. pR Raw data which is not converted or interpreted in any way by Uniface. The byte values 0 through 255 must be supported. pLC The same as pC. Uniface uses pLC as a possible packing code for the last field containing the variable part of the record. pLU The same as pU. Uniface uses pLU as a possible packing code for the last field containing the variable part of the record. pLR The same as pR. Uniface uses pLR as a possible packing code for the last field containing the variable part of the record. pN1-pN32 Number as ASCII string. The lengths of pN1 through pN32 are one through 32 characters. Format: Right-aligned Sign left The decimal point is included By default, leading zeros. If uinfo->supzero is set to a nonzero value, leading spaces are used. Packing codes pN1 through pN32 can contain an exponent, in scientific notation, if the data type is F. This is optional. pP1-pP16 The packing codes pP1 through pP8 are packed decimal numeric strings (one digit per ‘nibble’), with the sign encoded in the first nibble. The lengths of pP1 through pP8 are one through eight bytes. The hexadecimal value for the plus sign ( + ) is A, C, E or F. The hexadecimal value for the minus sign ( - ) is B or D. For example, packing code pP2 can represent -999 through 999. The packing codes pP9 through pP16 are reserved for future use. pQ1-pQ16 pQ1 through pQ8 are the same as pP1 through pP8, but the sign is encoded in the last nibble. The packing codes pQ9 through pQ16 are reserved for future use. pI1-pI4, pI8 The pI packing codes are signed binary integers in the native byte order and native integer encoding. pI1 through pI4 are one- through four- byte integers. pI8 is an eight-byte integer. The pI packing codes are the natural integer representation on most hardware architectures, but not necessarily the natural storage format for DBMSs. pI3 and pI8 are generally not supported by DBMSs. pF4, pF8 The pF4 packing code is a four-byte floating point number in the native encoding. The pF8 packing code is a double-precision eight-byte floating point number in the native encoding. pG8 The DEC VAX eight-byte double precision G_floating. pD1-pD16 The packing codes in the table represent a Date: Table 1. Packing codes that represent a Date Packing code Encoding Format Length pD1 ASCII DD-MMM-YYYY 11 characters pD2 ASCII YYYYMMDD 8 characters pD3 ASCII DDMMYYYY 8 characters pD4 ASCII YYMMDD 6 characters pD5 ASCII DDMMYY 6 characters pD6 Binary YYMD 4 bytes pD7 Binary DMYY 4 bytes pD8 Binary YMD 3 bytes pD9 Binary DMY 3 bytes pD10 Binary YYMMDD 6 bytes pD11 Binary DDMMYY 6 bytes pD12 ASCII MM/DD/YYYY 10 characters The lengths for the packing codes pD1 through pD13 are available as symbolic values sD1 through sD13 in the unidbm.h include file provided with Uniface. For ASCII codes, the length is positive, for binary codes it is negative. The packing codes pD14 through pD16 are reserved for future use. The packing code pD2 can be used to sort Dates alphabetically. pD12 is the PROGRESS SQLDATE format. The range of supported values is 1/1/1 through 12/31/9999. pT1-pT16 The packing codes in the table represent a Time or a combined Datetime: Table 2. Packing codes that represent a Date Packing code Encoding Format Length pT1 ASCII HH:NN:SS 8 characters pT2 ASCII HHMMSS 6 characters pT3 ASCII DDMMYYYY HHMMSS 15 characters pT4 ASCII HH:MM:SS 8 characters pT5 ASCII HH:MM:SS.S 10 characters pT6 ASCII HH:MM:SS.SS 11 characters The lengths for the packing codes pT1 through pT6 are available as symbolic values sT1 through sT6 in the unidbm.h include file provided with Uniface. For ASCII codes, the length is positive, for binary codes it is negative. The packing codes pT7 through pT16 are reserved for future use. The packing code pT2 can be used to sort Times alphabetically. pE1-pE16 The packing codes in the table represent a combined Datetime: Table 3. Combined Datetime packing codes Packing Encoding Datetime format Length pE1 Binary Sybase linear 8 bytes pE3 ASCII DDMMYYYY HH:MM:SS 17 characters pE4 ASCII DDMMYYYY HHMMSS 15 characters pE5 ASCII DD-MMM-YYYY HH:MM:SS 25 characters pE6 Binary ORACLE internal 7 bytes pE7 u Sybase external, format MM/DD/YYYY HH:MM:SS.TT 22 characters pE8 ASCII YYYYMMDD HHMMSS 15 characters pE9 Binary Sybase small datetime pE10 ASCII YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS 19 characters pE11 ASCII YYYY-MM-DD:HH:MM:SS 19 characters pE12 ASCII YYYY-MM-DD:HH:MM:SS.S 21 characters pE13 ASCII YYYY-MM-DD:HH:MM:SS.SS 22 characters The lengths for the packing codes pE1 through pE13 are available as symbolic values sE1 through sE13 in the unidbm.h include file provided with Uniface. For ASCII codes, the length is positive, for binary codes it is negative. The unused packing codes in the range pE14 through pE16 are reserved for future use. The value in a pE5 is appended with spaces to fill up the 25 available positions. pE9 (Sybase only) is a four-byte structure consisting of two unsigned two-byte binary integers in the native integer encoding. The first integer encodes the number of days since 1/1/1900. The second integer encodes the number of minutes since midnight. pB1-pB8 The packing codes in the table represent a Boolean value: Table 4. Packing codes that represent a Boolean Packing code Encoding Format Length pB1 ASCII 0 or 1 1 character pB2 ASCII F or T 1 character pB3 ASCII N or Y 1 character pB4 Binary 0 or 1 1 byte pB5 Binary 0 or -1 2 bytes The lengths for the packing codes pB1 through pB5 are available as symbolic values sB1 through sB5 in the unidbm.h include file provided with Uniface. For ASCII codes, the length is positive, for binary codes it is negative. pB5 is the PROGRESS SQLBOOL format. It is a two-byte signed binary integer in the native integer encoding, in which 0 represents TRUE and -1 FALSE. The packing codes pB6 through pB8 are reserved for future use. pM1-pM8 The packing codes in the table are for the Money data type: Table 5. Packing codes for the Money type Packing code Description pM1 Eight-byte signed integer. pM2 Eight-byte double precision float. pM4 Sybase internal Money format. pM6 Sybase small Money format. The packing codes pM3, pM5, pM7 and pM8 are reserved for future use. pM4 is an eight-byte structure consisting of two four-byte integers. The first four-byte integer is signed and the most significant, the second four-byte integer is unsigned and the least significant. pM6 (Sybase only) is a four-byte unsigned binary integer in the native integer encoding. The range of supported values is -214748.3648 through 214748.3647 with an accuracy of up to a ten-thousandth of a unit. pJ1-pJ32 Number as an ASCII string. The lengths of pJ1 through pJ32 are one through 32 characters. Format: Right-aligned Sign left The decimal point is not included Leading zeros pO1-pO32 The zoned numeric encoding of the OpenVMS VAX trailing numeric string. A value with packing code O i is a sequence of i bytes. The first byte is the most significant digit. Every byte, with the exception of the last byte, is a digit encoded as an ASCII decimal digit character (0 through 9). The last byte encodes both the least significant digit and the sign: the ASCII decimal digits 0 through 9 represent an unsigned digit, the ASCII characters ‘p’ through ‘y’ represent -0 through -9. For example, packing code O2 can represent the integer values -99 through 99. pY1-pY32 A user-defined packing code for the Numeric data type. The connector can handle the user-defined packing code in the following ways: Use it to support a DBMS storage format which is not covered by the standard packing code set. In this case, the user-defined conversion routines must be implemented. Redefine the packing code to a pR packing code which maps to a raw storage format. pZ1-pZ8 A user-defined packing code for the Date, Time or Datetime data type. See also the explanation for pY1 through pY32. Related Links Packing Codes Packing codes are mappings between Uniface data types and DBMS data types (storage formats). When writing a field, Uniface passes the data and a valid packing code to the DBMS connector, which uses the packing code to determine the appropriate storage format for storing the data in the target DBMS. Packing codes also determine how data is interfaced with 3GL routines. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
